name: Tuvalu

colors.symbolism: the Pacific Ocean and the sky over Tuvalu
the nine stars representing the country's islands; the sun and happiness
the cross of St George and saltire of St Patrick within the Union Flag
the saltire and cross fimbriations of the Union Flag

description: A light-blue field with the Union Flag in the upper-hoist canton (one quarter of the flag) and nine yellow five-pointed stars scattered across the fly half in a pattern reflecting the geographical arrangement of Tuvalu's inhabited islands.
history: Originally adopted on 1 October 1978, the day Tuvalu became independent from the United Kingdom, with the nine stars arranged on a light-blue field and the Union Flag in the canton. Replaced on 1 October 1995 by a markedly different design without the Union Flag, but the original was restored — with minor adjustments — on 11 April 1997 after political controversy. The 1997 design positioned the stars to better reflect the actual geography of the islands.

features.symbolism: a light sky-blue field representing the Pacific Ocean, on which Tuvalu's nine inhabited islands are arranged in a map-like pattern of stars
Tuvalu's continuing membership of the Commonwealth and status as a Commonwealth realm under the British Crown

features.role: nine yellow stars in the map-like pattern of Tuvalu's islands
features.symbolism: the nine inhabited islands of Tuvalu — Nanumea, Nanumanga, Niutao, Nui, Vaitupu, Nukufetau, Funafuti, Nukulaelae and Niulakita — arranged geographically as they appear on a map of the country, with the hoist edge oriented to the west
